subject: ap-chemistry
title: Acid-Base Titration & Calorimetry
stimulus: 1. A student is studying the reaction between lactic acid, $C_3H_6O_3$, and sodium hydroxide, NaOH, as represented in the balanced equation: $C_3H_6O_3$(aq) + NaOH(aq) → $NaC_3H_5O_3$(aq) + $H_2O$(l)

parts.prompt:
The structural formula of lactic acid is shown in the following diagram. Circle the hydrogen atom that most
readily participates in the chemical reaction with sodium hydroxide.
parts.scoring_notes: The most acidic H is on the –COOH group (carboxylic acid). Do not accept H on the –OH alcohol group.

scoring_points.description: For circling the carboxylic acid H (–COOH): 1 point

required_elements: The H on the –COOH (carboxylic acid) group is circled.
alternatives.correct_example: The carboxylic acid H (–COOH) is the most acidic because the conjugate base (carboxylate, –COO⁻) is stabilized by resonance. This H most readily reacts with the strong base NaOH.

wrong_examples: Circling an H on –OH (alcohol) — the alcohol O–H is far less acidic than carboxylic acid O–H.
Circling C–H hydrogens — C–H bonds are not acidic under these conditions.
common_traps: Lactic acid has both –COOH and –OH groups; only the carboxylic acid H reacts readily with a strong base.

parts.prompt:
The student begins the experiment by dissolving 10.22 g of sodium hydroxide (molar mass 40.00 g / mol) in
enough water to produce 500. mL of solution. Calculate the molarity of the sodium hydroxide solution.

The student uses the sodium hydroxide solution from part (b), a buret, a pH meter, and a 100 mL Erlenmeyer
flask to titrate a 25.0 mL sample of lactic acid solution. The student's data are shown in the following graph.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated molarity of NaOH: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{NaOH}) = \frac{10.22\text{ g}}{40.00\text{ g/mol}} = 0.2555\text{ mol}$ 
$M = \frac{0.2555\text{ mol}}{0.500\text{ L}} = 0.511\text{ M}$

wrong_examples: Dividing by 500 (mL) instead of 0.500 L — units mismatch gives an answer 1000× too small.
common_traps: Always convert mL to L before calculating molarity.

parts.prompt:
Use the information in the graph to determine the approximate pK of lactic acid. ____________
a
parts.scoring_notes: pKa is read at the half-equivalence point of the titration curve (pH at half the equivalence volume). Acceptable range: 3.7–4.0.

scoring_points.description: For the correct pKa read from the graph (acceptable range 3.7–4.0): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: The pKa is found at the half-equivalence point, where [HA] = [A⁻] and the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation gives pH = pKa. From the graph, the half-equivalence point occurs at approximately half the equivalence volume, at pH ≈ 3.9.

wrong_examples: Reading the pH at the equivalence point — that gives the pH of the salt, not pKa.
Reading the initial pH — that gives the pH of the pure acid solution.
common_traps: pKa = pH at the half-equivalence point (Henderson–Hasselbalch: when [A⁻]/[HA] = 1, log term = 0, pH = pKa).

parts.prompt:
The preceding diagram represents the relative amounts of major species in a sample of the solution in the flask
at one point during the titration. (Note that water molecules are omitted.)
(i)Draw an X on the preceding titration curve at a point in the titration where the reaction mixture would
be represented by this diagram.
(ii)Justify your answer.

Experiment Mass of NaOH(s ) (grams) Volume of Solution (mL) Titration Curve
1 10.22 500. Already shown on graph
2 20.44 500. ?
(iii)The student repeats the experiment but uses a solution of NaOH(aq) with twice the concentration, as
shown in the preceding table. On the following graph, draw the titration curve that would be expected
for experiment 2.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (d)(i)+(ii): X must be before the equivalence point where more acid particles than conjugate base particles are shown (between 3–8 mL). Part (d)(iii): equivalence point at 8 mL (half the volume needed with twice the concentration); same initial pH and shape; sharper rise at 8 mL.

scoring_points.description: For placing X at a volume ≥ 3 mL and < 8 mL (before the equivalence point): 1 point

required_elements: X is placed at a volume between 3 mL and 8 mL on the titration curve.
alternatives.correct_example: The diagram shows more acid than conjugate base, so the titration is less than halfway to the equivalence point. The X should be placed before the half-equivalence point (at less than half the equivalence volume).

wrong_examples: Placing X at or after the equivalence point — that would indicate mostly conjugate base present.
common_traps: If more HA than A⁻ is present, the titration is below the half-equivalence point.

scoring_points.description: For a correct justification based on relative particle counts: 1 point

required_elements: More acid particles (HA) than conjugate base particles (A⁻) are present in the diagram.
This means the titration has not reached the half-equivalence point, so the X must be at a volume less than half the equivalence volume.
alternatives.correct_example: The diagram shows a greater number of acid (HA) particles than conjugate base (A⁻) particles, indicating that more than half of the original acid remains unreacted. This corresponds to a point before the half-equivalence point on the titration curve.

wrong_examples: Referring to pH alone without mentioning particle counts from the diagram.

scoring_points.description: For drawing a curve with the equivalence point at 8 mL (half the original volume): 1 point

required_elements: Equivalence point is at 8 mL (half the original 16 mL equivalence volume, since concentration is doubled).
alternatives.correct_example: With twice the NaOH concentration, only half the volume is needed to neutralize the same amount of acid. The equivalence point shifts from 16 mL to 8 mL.

wrong_examples: Drawing the equivalence point at 16 mL — that is for experiment 1 with the lower concentration.
common_traps: Doubling the NaOH concentration halves the volume required to reach the equivalence point.

scoring_points.description: For a curve with appropriate initial pH and correct overall shape: 1 point

required_elements: Curve begins at the same initial pH as experiment 1 (same acid, same concentration).
Curve rises sharply at 8 mL (equivalence point) and levels off at high pH.
alternatives.correct_example: The initial pH is the same as experiment 1 (same lactic acid solution). The curve rises gradually, has an inflection point at 8 mL, then levels off at high pH — same general sigmoidal shape but shifted left.

wrong_examples: Starting at a different initial pH — the acid solution is unchanged.
Drawing a curve that shows no steep rise (no visible equivalence point).

parts.prompt:
In a third experiment, the student investigates the enthalpy of the reaction between lactic acid and sodium
hydroxide. The student combines 100.0 mL of a 0.500 M lactic acid solution at 20.0°C with 100.0 mL of a
0.500 M NaOH solution at 20.0°C in a calorimeter. The final temperature of the resulting combined solution
is 23.2°C. Assume that the density of each solution before combining is 1.00 g / mL and that the specific heat
capacity of the combined solution is 4.2 J / (g · ° C).
(i)Calculate the quantity of heat produced in the reaction, in J.

(ii)Calculate the molar enthalpy of reaction, in kJ/ mol . Include the sign in your answer.
rxn
(iii)The student claims that if heat is lost from the calorimeter to the surrounding air during the reaction,
then the experimental value of the molar enthalpy of reaction will be smaller in magnitude than the
actual value. Do you agree or disagree with the student's claim? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (e)(i): q = mcΔT; total mass = 200.0 g. Part (e)(ii): moles of limiting reactant = 0.0500 mol; ΔH = −q/n (negative, exothermic). Part (e)(iii): agree — heat lost to surroundings means the measured q is less than the actual q, so |ΔH_rxn| measured is smaller.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated heat q (in J): 1 point
wrong_examples: Using mass = 100.0 g instead of 200.0 g.
common_traps: The combined solution has mass = sum of both volumes × density = 200.0 g total.

scoring_points.description: For the correct molar enthalpy with correct sign (negative): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n = (0.500)(0.1000) = 0.0500\text{ mol}$ 
$\Delta H_{rxn} = \frac{-q}{n} = \frac{-2700\text{ J}}{0.0500\text{ mol}} = -54000\text{ J/mol} = -54\text{ kJ/mol}$

wrong_examples: Reporting a positive ΔH — the temperature increased, indicating an exothermic reaction.
Using n = 0.500 mol (using molarity not moles).
common_traps: Because the reaction is exothermic (T increases), ΔH_rxn must be negative.

scoring_points.description: For 'agree' with valid calorimetric justification: 1 point

required_elements: If heat is lost from the calorimeter, the measured temperature increase is less than the actual temperature increase.
A smaller measured q gives a smaller calculated |ΔH_rxn|.
alternatives.correct_example: Agree. If heat escapes to the surroundings, the temperature rise of the solution ($\Delta T$) is less than it would be in a perfectly insulated calorimeter. Since $q = mc\Delta T$, the measured q is smaller, so the calculated $|\Delta H_{rxn}| = q/n$ is also smaller than the true value.

wrong_examples: Disagree — heat loss always reduces the measured q, making |ΔH| smaller, not larger.
common_traps: Heat lost to surroundings = less heat measured by solution = smaller q measured = underestimated |ΔH|.
